summary: DHH recounts his experience at Caput, where the company invested over $25k in salary plus training and equipment based on a one-hour interview and an unchecked resume. Despite this significant trust in hiring, the company required a week-long approval process involving four people to authorize a $40 software purchase. The bureaucratic ordeal was so demoralizing that DHH stopped trying to improve his productivity through better tools. He argues this represents a fundamental misallocation of trust that is both demoralizing and bad for business.
key_points: Caput made a $25k+ hiring investment based on minimal vetting — a one-hour interview with no reference checks
The same company required a week and four approvals to authorize a $40 software purchase
The bureaucratic friction caused DHH to stop seeking productivity improvements on company time
Companies should invest heavily in vetting employees upfront but then trust their judgment on small decisions
money_quotes: It basicly all came down to trust. They trusted that Caput would derive more value from my work than what they had to pay to get it.
They didn't even trust me to purchase a $40 piece of development software on my own accord.
It took a small week, the review of four people, and an attempt to dissuade me from spending company cash on shareware, if the task could be solved in another way, to make it happen.
After that ordeal, I ceased trying to improve my productivity through better tools (at least on company time).
Go the whole nine yards to make sure your $25k+ investments are solid, but don't question your employees over petty cash. It's demoralizing and bad for business.
key_insight: Companies that trust employees enough to hire them on minimal vetting but won't trust them with petty cash decisions create a perverse incentive structure that kills productivity and morale.
